segments.from: Burlington, Iowa (connection from Chicago via the Chicago, Burlington and Quincy Railroad)
segments.cost: $8-$15 emigrant fare from Burlington; $3-$5 'land-explorer' excursion rate (1870s-1880s). The railroad offered free or reduced-fare 'land-looking' tickets to prospective settlers, refundable against land purchase.
segments.conditions: The line crossed the Missouri River at Plattsmouth, Nebraska and ran west through rolling prairie that the railroad's colonization department advertised as the finest agricultural land in America. Emigrant trains in the 1870s-1880s carried entire communities — Bohemian families from Chicago, Scandinavian families from Minnesota, German Mennonites from Russia — who had been recruited as groups by railroad land agents. The agents met immigrants at Castle Garden or Ellis Island, escorted them to emigrant cars, and accompanied them to the land office at Lincoln or Grand Island, where they signed papers for railroad land before they had seen it.

segments.to: Denver, Colorado (via the Burlington and Missouri River Railroad in Nebraska, later consolidated into the CB&Q system)
segments.cost: $12-$20 emigrant fare; railroad land grants along this stretch sold at $2.50-$8.00 per acre on credit terms (10 years at 6% interest), deliberately priced to compete with free Homestead Act land by offering better locations — closer to the railroad, closer to water, closer to towns.
segments.conditions: West of Lincoln, the railroad crossed the Platte River valley — the same route the overland emigrants had followed by wagon a generation earlier. The landscape grew drier and flatter with every mile, the grass shorter, the trees fewer. At Grand Island and Kearney, settlers disembarked into towns that existed because the railroad had put them there — platted, named, and sold by the railroad's town-lot department. Beyond the 100th meridian, rainfall dropped below the 20 inches needed for reliable dryland farming, and settlers who had been promised 'rain follows the plow' discovered that it did not. Dust storms, grasshopper plagues (1874-1877), and drought drove many back east within a few years.

nicknames: The Q (after consolidation into the CB&Q)
who_used_it: Homestead Act settlers from the Midwest and directly from Europe, recruited by the most aggressive immigration department of any American railroad. The Burlington employed agents in Sweden, Norway, Denmark, Germany, England, and Bohemia (Czech lands) who distributed pamphlets in local languages, placed newspaper advertisements, and organized group emigration of entire villages. Czech and Bohemian communities in southeastern Nebraska (Wilber, Crete, Prague), Swedish settlements along the Republican River, and German-Russian Mennonite colonies in central Nebraska all trace their origins to Burlington and Missouri River Railroad recruitment. The railroad sold approximately 2.5 million acres of its federal land grant in Nebraska between 1870 and 1900.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} left {origin} on a Burlington emigrant car, riding toward {destination} on a ticket that the railroad's agent had sold alongside a promise and a pamphlet full of Nebraska wheat fields — and a land deed that would be waiting at the end of the line, ten years of payments, and the question of whether twenty inches of rain would come.
source_references: Richard C. Overton, 'Burlington West: A Colonization History of the Burlington Railroad' (Harvard University Press, 1941)
Frederick C. Luebke, 'Immigrants and Politics: The Germans of Nebraska, 1880-1900' (University of Nebraska Press, 1969)
David Emmons, 'Garden in the Grasslands: Boomer Literature of the Central Great Plains' (University of Nebraska Press, 1971)
Bruce E. Nicoll, 'Nebraska: A Pictorial History' (University of Nebraska Press, 1967)
